records.ety: [F., fr. LL. signale, fr. L. signum. See Sign, n.]

defs: A sign made for the purpose of giving notice to a person of some occurrence, command, or danger; also, a sign, event, or watchword, which has been agreed upon as the occasion of concerted action.
quotes: All obeyed The wonted signal and superior voice Of this great potentate.

defs: A token; an indication; a foreshadowing; a sign; anything taken as evidence of some process.
quotes: The weary sun . . . Gives signal of a goodly day to-morrow.
There was not the least signal of the calamity to be seen.

defs: A measurable electrical quantity, such as voltage or current, that conveys information by varying in magnitude over time; as, the signals from the strongest commercial radio stations can be received over hundreds of miles.
Noticeable; distinguished from what is ordinary; eminent; remarkable; memorable; as, a signal exploit; a signal service; a signal act of benevolence.
quotes: As signal now in low, dejected state As erst in highest, behold him where he lies.

defs: Of or pertaining to signals, or the use of signals in conveying information; as, a signal flag or officer.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Signaled (<?/) Signalled; p. pr. & vb. n. Signaling or Signalling.]

defs: To communicate by signals; as, to signal orders.
To notify by a signals; to make a signal or signals to; as, to signal a fleet to anchor.
